# The Effect of Remote Work Policies on Organizational Trust: A Mixed-Methods Study

**Authors:** E. Synthetic, F. Fictional, G. Testcase

**Keywords:** remote work, organizational trust, mixed methods, survey, thematic analysis, COVID-19 aftermath

## Abstract

This study examines the relationship between remote work policies and
organizational trust in mid-size technology companies. Using a sequential
mixed-methods design, we first conducted a survey (N=412) measuring
organizational trust across three dimensions (competence trust, benevolence
trust, and integrity trust) using validated scales. We then conducted 24
semi-structured interviews with managers and employees to explore the
mechanisms through which remote work policies affect trust. Quantitative
results show a significant positive association between flexible remote work
policies and benevolence trust (beta=0.34, p<0.001), a weaker association
with competence trust (beta=0.12, p=0.04), and no significant association
with integrity trust. Qualitative findings reveal four mechanisms: reduced
surveillance perception, increased autonomy signaling, communication quality
changes, and boundary management challenges. We discuss implications for
organizational design and HR policy.

## 1. Introduction

The rapid adoption of remote work during and after the COVID-19 pandemic has
fundamentally altered the landscape of organizational behavior research. While
early studies focused on productivity and job satisfaction, the question of how
remote work arrangements affect the deeper structure of organizational
relationships — particularly trust — has received less systematic attention.

## 2. Literature Review

Organizational trust has been conceptualized along multiple dimensions. Mayer,
Davis, and Schoorman (1995) identify ability, benevolence, and integrity as
the three components of trustworthiness. Subsequent work has refined these
dimensions in various organizational contexts (Colquitt et al., 2007;
Schoorman et al., 2007).

Remote work research has expanded significantly since 2020. Allen et al.
(2015) provide a comprehensive review of telecommuting research. More recent
studies have examined the pandemic's impact on remote work adoption (Barrero
et al., 2021) and its effects on collaboration (Yang et al., 2022).

## 3. Methods

### 3.1 Research Design

We employed a sequential explanatory mixed-methods design (Creswell & Plano
Clark, 2018). The quantitative phase preceded the qualitative phase to
identify patterns that were then explored in depth through interviews.

### 3.2 Quantitative Phase

**Sample:** 412 employees from 8 mid-size technology companies (100-500
employees) recruited through professional networks.

**Measures:** Organizational Trust Inventory (OTI; Cummings & Bromiley, 1996),
Remote Work Policy Scale (developed for this study, alpha=0.89), demographic
controls.

**Analysis:** Hierarchical multiple regression with clustered standard errors
at the company level.

### 3.3 Qualitative Phase

**Sample:** 24 semi-structured interviews (12 managers, 12 employees) selected
through purposive sampling from the survey respondents.

**Analysis:** Reflexive thematic analysis (Braun & Clarke, 2019) with
independent coding by two researchers.

## 4. Results

### 4.1 Quantitative Findings

Remote work policy flexibility was significantly associated with benevolence
trust (beta=0.34, p<0.001) and competence trust (beta=0.12, p=0.04), but not
integrity trust (beta=0.03, p=0.52). Company size and industry sector were
not significant moderators.

### 4.2 Qualitative Findings

Four themes emerged from the qualitative analysis:

1. **Reduced surveillance perception:** Employees interpreted flexible policies
   as signals that management trusts them, which reciprocally increased their
   trust in management.
2. **Autonomy signaling:** The ability to choose work location was perceived
   as a concrete demonstration of organizational respect for employee judgment.
3. **Communication quality:** Remote work required more deliberate communication,
   which some participants experienced as more transparent and others as more
   superficial.
4. **Boundary management:** Difficulty separating work and personal life under
   remote arrangements created stress that, for some participants, undermined
   trust in the organization's concern for their wellbeing.

## 5. Discussion

Our findings suggest that the relationship between remote work and trust is
multidimensional and operates through distinct mechanisms for different trust
components. The strong association with benevolence trust aligns with signaling
theory: flexible policies signal organizational care.

## 6. Conclusion

Remote work policies affect organizational trust, but primarily through the
benevolence dimension. Organizations implementing remote work should attend to
the trust-signaling properties of their policies, not just their logistical
efficiency.

- Barrero, J. M., Bloom, N., & Davis, S. J. (2021). Why working from home will stick. NBER Working Paper 28731.
- Braun, V., & Clarke, V. (2019). Reflecting on reflexive thematic analysis. Qualitative Research in Sport, Exercise and Health, 11(4), 589-597.
- Colquitt, J. A., Scott, B. A., & LePine, J. A. (2007). Trust, trustworthiness, and trust propensity. Journal of Applied Psychology, 92(4), 909-927.
- Creswell, J. W., & Plano Clark, V. L. (2018). Designing and Conducting Mixed Methods Research (3rd ed.). SAGE.
- Cummings, L. L., & Bromiley, P. (1996). The Organizational Trust Inventory. In R. Kramer & T. Tyler (Eds.), Trust in Organizations. SAGE.
- Mayer, R. C., Davis, J. H., & Schoorman, F. D. (1995). An integrative model of organizational trust. Academy of Management Review, 20(3), 709-734.
- Schoorman, F. D., Mayer, R. C., & Davis, J. H. (2007). An integrative model of organizational trust: Past, present, and future. Academy of Management Review, 32(2), 344-354.
